parts.text: 7 (a) With reference to Figure 1, calculate the index number for international visitors to Taiwan from China in 2019, using 2015 as the base year. You are advised to show your working. (2) (b) Analy se two reasons why ‘Taiwanese firms moved to China to set up their manufacturing facilities’ (Extract A, lines 4–5). (6) (c) Explain what is meant by a ‘tariff ’ (Extract A, line 7). (4) (d) W
parts.answer: Application 2 Quantitative skills assessed: QS5: Calculate and interpret index numbers. QS9: Interpret, apply and analyse information in written, graphical, tabular and numerical forms. Up to 2 marks for calculations: • 2.71/4.18 (1) • x 100 = 64.8 (1) N.B. Award 2 marks for the correct answer (2) Question Analyse two reasons why ‘Taiwanese firms moved to China to set up their manufacturing facilities’ (Extract A, lines 4-5). Mark

Knowledge 2, Application 2, Analysis 2 Quantitative skills assessed: QS9: Interpret, apply and analyse information in written, graphical, tabular and numerical forms. Knowledge Up to 2 marks for knowledge of two reasons: Taiwanese firms have: • access to cheap labour (1) • access to a large Chinese market (1) Application Up to 2 marks for application to the reasons: • China is Taiwan’s most important trading partner (1) • China is main destination for Taiwan’s exports (1) • Much of Taiwan’s foreign direct investment also goes to China (1) • Over 100 000 Taiwanese firms have moved to China (1) (6) • An accurately labelled cost/revenue diagram showing change in revenue or costs (1) and increase in profits of Taiwanese firms (1) Analysis 1 mark for a linked explanation of each reason (1+1): • Lower cost of production for firms, lower export prices and increase in international competitiveness (1) • Greater customer base and therefore higher revenue/sales and market share (1) Question Explain what is meant by a ‘tariff’ (Extract A, line 7). Mark

Knowledge 2, Application 2 Quantitative skills assessed: QS9: Interpret, apply and analyse information in written, graphical, tabular and numerical forms. Knowledge Up to 2 marks for understanding of a tariff, e.g.: • A tax (1) on imported goods (1) 1 mark for identification that tariff is a type of restriction on free trade/protectionist policy Application Up to 2 marks for application to a tariff, e.g.: • US imposed tariffs on several Chinese-made goods in 2018 (1) • The average tariff is 19.3% on US imports from China (1) • An accurately labelled tariff diagram (2) (4) Question With reference to the information provided and your own knowledge, examine two likely problems arising from the fall in the number of Chinese tourists to Taiwan. Mark Quantitative skills assessed: QS2: Calculate, use and understand percentages, percentage changes and percentage point changes. QS9: Interpret, apply and analyse information in written, graphical, tabular and numerical forms. Knowledge and analysis 1 mark for knowledge of each problem (1+1) and 1 mark for linked analysis (1+1), e.g.: • Less tourists spending on goods/services provided in the local economy (1) will not help fill the foreign exchange gap/lower foreign exchange (1) • Less FDI by TNCs in Taiwan (1) less investment in infrastructure, hotels, associated services (1) • Lower injections could lead to slower economic growth (1) not creating enough employment: tourism is a labour-intensive industry (1) • Less tax revenue for the government from lower expenditure by the tourists (1) available to reduce inequality through income redistribution/preserve national heritage (1) Application Up to 2 marks for application to sources, e.g.: • Figure 1: Taiwan had only 2.71 million Chinese tourists in 2019 (1) down from 4.18 million in 2015 (1) Evaluation Up to 2 marks for evaluative comments (1+1 or 2+0): • Problems are likely to be significant (1) as there has been a 35% fall in tourist numbers (1) • There might not be a fall in

Indicative content guidance Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make, but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. QS9: Interpret, apply and analyse information in written, graphical, tabular and numerical forms. Knowledge, Application and Analysis (8 marks) – indicative content • “incentives that made it easier to buy land and register property” – reducing bureaucracy/regulatory burden on firms simplifies the process of buying land and gaining environmental clearances. It otherwise hinders entrepreneurship in the private sector by adding to a firm’s cost of production • “investment in infrastructure to ensure the continuous provision of gas, electricity and water supply” – improvements in infrastructure will help to address the bottlenecks in power sources and water supply. It ensures the stability of electricity (no power outages) and no water shortages. This will help raise total factor productivity through fall in costs for the private sector, thereby allowing more efficient use of productive inputs • “subsidies and tax incentives for the technology sector” – will reduce cost of production for firms and may encourage firms to invest in innovation which would not otherwise be started due to the high risk and cost involved. This allows the firms to gain technical economies of scale and dynamic efficiency. It helps reduce problem of lack of finance to fund research and development • Improved trade links with countries in both South Asia and Southeast Asia – allows for trade creation with countries in the NSP leading to increased specialisation and trade through comparative advantage. Countries in the NSP accounted for around 20% of Taiwan’s exports in 2019 Level Mark Descriptor 0 No rewardable material. Level 1 1–3 Displays isolated, superficial or imprecise
